coredata.dc:title: Fabrication and characterization of microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots crosslinked sodium alginate hydrogel films
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Pengyuan
Li, Xin
Kong, Baohua
Zhu, Ying-ao
Wang, Meihui
Wang, Hui
Liu, Qian
coredata.dc:description: In this study, potassium-incorporated carbon dots (K-CDs) and nitrogen-incorporated carbon dots (N-CDs) were composted using the microwave-assisted method, in which the carbon source is citric acid. Subsequently, the prepared CDs were added into sodium alginate (NaAlg)/CaCO3 to form a hydrogel film. The Ca2+ in the system is tend to be released in the presence of acidic CDs to promote the cross-linking of NaAlg. This study presents a NaAlg hydrogel film preparation process that requires no additional acid and is natural and environmentally friendly. Moreover, it gives the NaAlg hydrogel film excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties and also improves its mechanical properties and gel strength. The release behaviors of the CDs in the hydrogel films were also explored. The prepared CD-incorporated NaAlg hydrogel films have potential applications in medical, biological engineering, food preservation, and other fields owing to their functional properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NaAlg
Carbon dots
Hydrogel film
Antimicrobial property
Antioxidant property

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 32302129 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province ( LH2021C048 ), and the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Financial Assistance ( LBH-Z17214 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of (a) K-CDs and (b) N-CDs, UV–Vis spectra of (c) K-CDs and (d) N-CDs, and fluorescence spectra of (e) K-CDs and (f) N-CDs.
(a) DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities of the CDs, (b) inhibition zones of the CDs against E. coli and S. aureus, and (c) cytotoxicity of the CDs.
(A) FTIR spectra of the (a) ACk and (b) ACn hydrogel films and (B) fracture-surface morphology of samples subjected to freeze-drying cycles.
Release of CDs from AC films into food simulant: 3 % acetic acid (ab); 95 % ethanol(cd).
The water absorption rates of (a) K-CDs and (b) N-CDs and the DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activities of (c) K-CDs and (d) N-CDs.
(A) DSC behavior and (B) TGA curves of the NaAlg/CD and all AC films.

The growth inhibition rates (%) of (a) K-CD-incorporated and (b) N-CD-incorporated hydrogel films against E. coli and S. aureus and the growth curves of (c, e) E. coli and (d, f) S. aureus after exposure to K-CDs and N-CDs, respectively.
The release performance (diffusivity and diffusional exponent) of CDs in the films when immersed in the simulation solution.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Mean ± standard deviation values in the same column denoted with different letters are significantly different (P < 0.05). n, diffusion index; D, diffusion coefficient; K, equilibrium constant; R 2, coefficient of determination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanical and gel properties of the AC hydrogel films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: Mean ± standard deviation values in the same column denoted with different lowercase letters are significantly different (P < 0.05). TS, tensile strength; EB, elongation at break.

head.ce:title: Fabrication and characterization of microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots crosslinked sodium alginate hydrogel films

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sharable Platform of Large-Scale Instruments & Equipments, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sharable Platform of Large-Scale Instruments & Equipments

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sharable Platform of Large-Scale Instruments & Equipments, Northeast Agricultural University, Harbin, Heilongjiang, 150030, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, potassium-incorporated carbon dots (K-CDs) and nitrogen-incorporated carbon dots (N-CDs) were composted using the microwave-assisted method, in which the carbon source is citric acid. Subsequently, the prepared CDs were added into sodium alginate (NaAlg)/CaCO3 to form a hydrogel film. The Ca2+ in the system is tend to be released in the presence of acidic CDs to promote the cross-linking of NaAlg. This study presents a NaAlg hydrogel film preparation process that requires no additional acid and is natural and environmentally friendly. Moreover, it gives the NaAlg hydrogel film excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties and also improves its mechanical properties and gel strength. The release behaviors of the CDs in the hydrogel films were also explored. The prepared CD-incorporated NaAlg hydrogel films have potential applications in medical, biological engineering, food preservation, and other fields owing to their functional properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel as a three-dimensional network is composed of hydrophilic polymers and numerous water molecules. Hydrogels are ideal for wound dressing and other biomedical applications owing to their excellent hydrophilicity, good biocompatibility, high sensitivity to the physiological environment, and ability to provide a moist environment for the wound interface [1]. Natural polymers as a matrix for hydrogels, such as natural polysaccharides, are advantageous because they are reproducible and cause no pollution compared with synthetic polymers [2]. Sodium alginate (NaAlg) as a natural linear polymer in hydrogel film is widely applied in the field of biomedicine owing to its good biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-toxicity, low immunogenicity, and other excellent properties. However, the application of NaAlg hydrogel films is usually restricted owing to its poor mechanical properties [3,4]. This is due to the weaken carboxylate activity of NaAlg molecular chain, which affects its biocompatibility and application. Therefore, NaAlg hydrogel films are often reacted with other reagents with functional groups to improve the activity of the carboxyl group [5]. Moreover, the development of NaAlg hydrogel films with excellent antimicrobial and antioxidant properties has been widely studied owing to the urgent requirement in food and biological materials [6]. Despite NaAlg hydrogels possess excellent water absorption and water retention properties, they do not possess antimicrobial and antioxidant capabilities [7]. Currently, the commonly used antimicrobial agents are metal ions, plant essential oils and nanoparticle [8]. However, metal ions can lead to some harm to the environment, whereas essential oils are volatile and can cause irritation [9]. Various nonmetal antimicrobial nanofillers have been used to prepare biopolymer-based nanocomposite films [10]. Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel kind of nanomaterials with particle size of 2.0–10.0 nm. CDs have been superior modifiers of other materials in the fields of metal ion detection, bio-sensing, and bio-imaging owing to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, well chemical stability, excellent photoluminescence properties, and outstanding antimicrobial and antioxidant activities [11–13]. There is a wide variety of raw materials for preparing CDs, such as carbon-containing inorganic materials, carbon-containing organic small molecules and biomass resources [14]. CDs are usually synthesized through top-down or bottom-up methods [15]. Compared to bottom-up, top-down has some limitations, such as high cost of required materials, long preparation time, need for separate surface functionalization, and lower quantum yield [16,17]. Whereas bottom-up can be done during the preparation process and does not require further surface functionalization [18,19]. The microwave-assisted method as one of the bottom-up approaches allows CDs synthesis within minutes at ambient conditions using single step unlike hydrothermal methods. Furthermore, microwave-assisted method is a facile method for preparing fluorescent CDs and demands lower energy consumption compared with the conventional hydrothermal method [20]. In recent years, other hetero-atoms such as sulphur, nitrogen, and boron have been often incorporated into CDs to improve their performance [21]. Heteroatom doping is considered to be one of the most practical strategies to improve the performance of CDs. Compared with monoatomic doping of CDs, polyatomic doping can provide CDs with abundant functional groups, active sites, and surface defects, which can further enhance their performance [22]. Shi et al. prepared N,P-CDs by hydrothermal method using glucose, ammonia, and phosphoric acid as raw materials [23]. The introduction of nitrogen and phosphorus atoms could provide more active sites compared to the undoped heteroatoms [24]. CDs hydrogels exhibit a high degree of functional diversity due to their ease of synthesis and surface functionalization, luminescence, biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and low toxicity. CDs hydrogels have multidisciplinary applications in areas such as antimicrobial, drug delivery, bio-imaging, and environmental remediation [25,26]. Therefore, CDs were incorporated into NaAlg hydrogel films to improve the antimicrobial and antioxidant properties are very necessary. Nevertheless, the use of CDs in hydrogel films has been rarely reported.

In this study, a serials of doped carbon-dotted NaAlg hydrogel film with antimicrobial and antioxidant properties have been designed to address the problems of no antimicrobial and antioxidant properties and poor mechanical properties of Ca2+-crosslinked NaAlg hydrogel films. Potassium-incorporated carbon dots (K-CDs) and nitrogen-incorporated carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using the microwave-assisted synthesis method and added into NaAlg/CaCO3 polymer dope solution to obtain CDs-modified hydrogel film. The mechanical, structural, gel, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties of the synthesized NaAlg hydrogel film were expressed. Besides, the release behaviors of the two types of CDs in the hydrogel films were explored. The CD-incorporated NaAlg hydrogel films synthesized in this study have potential applications in medical, biological engineering, food preservation, and other fields.

NaAlg (AR: 98 %) and Urea were acquired from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid and K2HPO4 was purchased by R & M Chemicals (Suzhou, China). All the reagents were used as received without further purification.

K-CDs were devided into citric acid and K2HPO4. They are dissolved in equal quality (0.5 g) in aquadistillate. The mixing ratio is 5:1:10 as previously reported [27]. Subsequently, 10 mL of the above solution was stirred and reacted in a microwave oven (Midea M1-L213B, China) at 700 W for 180 s. The solid formed was uniformly dispersed in distilled water (50 mL) using a sonicator and then centrifuged at 5000 r for 10 min. Finally, it was purified by a 0.45-μm syringe strainer and freeze-dried to a powder in a freeze-dryer and set aside.

First, citric acid and urea powder in equal proportions were added into the distilled water, stirred until completely dissolved, and heated in a microwave oven (Midea) at 700 W for 180 s. Finally, the reaction product is cooled to 25 °C and then removed and dispersed in distilled water (20 mL) using a sonicator. Finally, the suspended solids were purified using a 0.45-μm syringe filter and freeze-dried to a powder.

First, 0.25 g CaCO3 was added to 100 mL deionized water at 25 °C to acquire 0.25 % (w/v) CaCO3 suspension. Then, 2 % (w/v) NaAlg solution was obtained adding 2 g NaAlg powder to 100 mL deionized water at 25 °C and mixed until thoroughly solubled. Glycerin in the same proportion as NaAlg was used as the plasticizer. The CDs powder was dissolved in distilled water to form a solution. Disparate concentrations of CDs (5 %, 7.5 %, 15 %, and 30 % w/w) were separately appended to the specimen. NaAlg hydrogel film without the addition of CDs was served as the control film and was labeled as AC-0. The film solutions were prepared with K-CDs or N-CDs and coded as ACk or ACn, respectively. The samples were labeled as ACk-5, ACk-7.5, ACk-15, ACk-30, ACn-5, ACn-7.5, ACn-15, and ACn-30, respectively. The obtained solutions were dumped on individual disposable plates (13 × 13 cm) and dried for 48 h at 60 °C. The NaAlg/CD films were peeled off from the disposable plates and stored at room temperature at 25 °C for further use.

The CDs samples were dropped on a copper grid coated with holey carbon film. The solvent was evaporated and the microstructure of CDs was observed using a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, 200 kV with Field Emission, HITACHI HT7800). It is worth noting that a carbon support film should be applied to the copper mesh [28].

The bonding configuration of the CDs was examined using FTIR (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, Germany) at 25 °C. The FTIR spectra were measured in the wavenumber scope of 400–4000 cm−1 with a scan rate of 64 s.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the CDs were analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (EVOLUTION201, China). Molecular energy level jumps in CDs was be determined by absorption peaks in the UV–Vis spectrum.
The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were analyzed using an fluorescence spectrophotometer (F7100, Hitachi, Japan).

The antioxidant activities of the CDs were estimated based on 2,2-diphenyl-1-pycrylhydrazyl (DPPH) and 2,2′-Azinobis- (3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6- sulphonate) (ABTS) radical scavenging rates using the means given by Zareie et al. [29]. It was determined at 517 nm and the radical scavenging activity was acquired using Eq. (1):
(1)
DPPH radical scavenging activity =
A 0
−
A 1
A 0
× 100 %
where A 0 is the absorbances of the control group and A 1 is the absorbances of the experimental group.

The ABTS radical scavenging activity was measured using the means given by Fu et al. [30]. The absorbance was obtained at 734 nm and the ABTS scavenging activity was calculated using Eq. (2):
(2)
ABTS scav e nging activity =
A 0
−
A 1
A 0
× 100 %
where A 0 and A 1 are the absorbances of the control and experimental groups, respectively.

The antimicrobial activities of CDs against E. coli and S. aureus were evaluated by the Oxford cup method. First, 1 mL of bacterial solution was mixed with sterilized nutrient agar. Once the bottom layer was solidified, it was placed in the Oxford cup. The nutrient agar was then reduced to a second layer, and after solidification the Oxford cups were removed and the added antimicrobial substance was added to the cup holes. The inoculated plate was incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and the antibacterial activity was assessed by determining the size of the inhibition zone.
To assess the biocompatibility of the prepared CDs, CCK-8 analysis was performed using NCM460 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the hydrogel film samples

ce:section.ce:para: The FTIR spectrum was determined using the way depicted in Section 2.3.1.1.

The morphology of the films was evaluated by a scanning electron microscope (S-3400N, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. Before the evaluation, all the samples were thinly coated with gold.

We carefully review the literature on food simulants and 3 % acetic acid and 95 % ethanol were chosen to study the release behavior of CDs from the films in foods [31,32]. To study the diffusion mechanism during the release of components, the release concentration of CDs from the AC hydrogel film was fitted using the Korsmeyer–Peppas model (Eq. (3)):
(3)
M
F , t
M
F , ∞
= k
t n
where M F,t (mg/kg) is the amount of functional component in the food simulation solution at time t (s), M F,∞ (mg/kg) is the amount of functional component in the food simulation solution at equilibrium, and k and n are the rate constant and diffusion index, respectively, for the release process. Therefore, if n > 0.5, the release process is considered to be an abnormal release, which does not defer to Fick's law of diffusion. If n < 0.5, the release process is considered to conform to Fick's law of diffusion [33].

The sample film (20 mm × 20 mm) was first intercepted and put into 20 mL 95 % ethanol and then placed in a shaker (37 °C, 65 rpm) for 10 h. The released medium (3 mL) was removed at a preconcerted gap and 3 mL ethanol was added. The absorbance of the released medium was surveyed at 204 nm using a UV-spectrophotometer to determine the amount of CDs released.

Simultaneously, on the basis of Fick's second law of diffusion, the release rate of CDs from the film to the simulated food solution was researched using Eq. (4):
(4)
M
F , t
M
F , ∞
= 1 −
∑
n = 0
∞
8
2 n + 1
2
π 2
exp
−
2 n + 1
2
π 2
L P 2
Dt
where M F,t (mg/kg) is the quantity of functional component in the food simulation solution at time t (s), M F,∞ (mg/kg) is the quantity of functional component in the food simulation solution at equilibrium, L p (m) is the thickness of the film, and k, D (m2 s−1), and n are the rate constant, diffusion index, and diffusion coefficient, respectively, for the release process. When M F,t/M F,∞ < 0.6, the following simplified equation (Eq. (5)) was used to calculate the diffusion coefficient:
(5)
M
F , t
M
F , ∞
=
2
L P
Dt π
0.5

The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were observed by a universal testing machine following the means given by Sun et al. [34]. The TS and EB were computed using Eq. (6) and Eq. (7), respectively:
(6)
TS
MPa
=
F
w × t
where F is the maximum force (N), w is the width (mm) of the film strip, and t is the thickness (mm) of the film.
(7)
EB
%
=
∆ L
L 0
where ΔL is the final length and the initial length (mm)'s interpolation and L 0 is the initial length.

The swelling rate of those samples was measured according to the method of Mayachiew et al. [35]. The samples were cut into rectangles of 10 mm × 30 mm. The samples were weighed and placed in a beaker of water. After that water was removed after 24 h of immersion and dried with filter paper and weighed. The water swelling ratio was computed using Eq. (8):
(8)
Swelling ratio
%
=
M s
−
M i
M i
× 100
where M i is the initial dry weight and M
s is the wet weight of a hydrogel film.

The gel strength (N) was measured using a TA.XT Plus Texture Analyzer (Stable Micro Systems, London, UK). All the hydrogel films were allowed to fully swell in water for 24 h at 37 °C before the test and later compressed using the probe of the texture analyser at a rate of 1 mm/s. The compression was terminated when the gels ruptured.

A NaAlg hydrogel film of m 0 dry weight and V 0 volume was put in a beaker of water and removed after soaking for different durations (t). The initial weight of the film is m 1 and the weight after water absorption is m
2 . The volume of the film is V
0 . Finally, the water absorption rate (R) was calculated using Eq. (9):
(9)
R =
m 1
−
m 0
V 0
t

The thermal behavior was analyzed using differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) (TA instruments, USA) [36]. The films (5 mg) were placed in an aluminium pan and heated from 20 to 170 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min in a nitrogen atmosphere. The thermal stability was recorded using a thermogravimetric analyser (TGA) (NETZSCH, Germany) at a constant heating rate (10 °C/min) from 30 to 600 °C in the nitrogen atmosphere.
The antioxidant activities of the hydrogel films were determined using the method described in Section 2.3.1.4.

The antimicrobial activities of the hydrogel films were studied in solution and on agar-LB plates. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) were used as the specific bacteria in our study. The antimicrobial activity of the AC-modified hydrogel films was determined using the plate colony counting method. First, all hydrogel films of each group were sterilized using a UV lamp for 1 h. Then, 100 μL bacterial suspension (about 108 CFU/mL) was added into a test tube involving 10 mL nutrient broth. Then, the hydrogel films were immersed in the test tubes and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Then, the quantity of viable bacteria in the medium was computed using the gradient dilution method. The growth inhibition rate was calculated using Eq. (10):
(10)
Bacterial inhibition rate
%
=
A − B
A
× 100 %
where A and B are the quantity of colonies in the medium without and with hydrogel films (CFU/mL), respectively.

All tests were worked in triplicate. The consequences are shown as mean ± standard deviation, and the data were analyzed using the SPSS 23.0 software (IBM Corp., Armonk, NY, USA). P < 0.05 was deemed to be meaningful.

TEM measurements were conducted in order to determine size of CDs. Fig. 1 shows the TEM image of the prepared hydrogel films. As can be seen in this Figure, the size of K-CDs and N-CDs is in the range of 1–3 nm with uniform size distribution. This size distribution is in agreement with that of traditional CDs that are synthesized through microwave assisted synthesis methods before [37]. The ultra-small particle size is favorable for obtaining a large specific surface area and providing more reaction sites [28].

To obtain message on the structure of the prepared CDs, the FTIR spectra of the CDs were obtained (Fig. 2(a) and (b)). As shown in Fig. 2(a), the characteristic peak for the stretching vibration of the OH bond appears at 3431 cm−1. The characteristic peak at 1709 cm−1 is derived from the stretching vibration of the CO group [38]. The vibration peak at 1392 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibration of CC. It is speculated that citric acid gradually carbonized to form a carbon core structure centered on CC during the process of microwave irradiation. The vibration peaks at 1152 cm−1 is corresponded to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C-O-C [39]. The FTIR spectrum in Fig. 2(b) shows the types of functional groups on the appearance of N-CDs. The broad peak observed at 3200 cm−1 is corresponded to OH and NH vibration stretching. The characteristic peaks are located at 1716 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1697 cm−1 (CC stretching), and 852 cm−1 (C-O-C stretching). The sharp peak at 1359 cm−1 is corresponded to the stretching vibration of CN, indicating that the nitrogen atom is successfully incorporated into the CDs.

The internal energy level jumps of carbon dot molecules can be analyzed by observing the absorption peaks in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 2c. The peak at 211 nm is corresponded to the n-π* transition of CO and the π-π* transition of CC in K-CDs [40]. The photograph of K-CDs dispersion under UV light shows a blue color (inset in Fig. 2c). The synthesized K-CDs are yellow in visible light and blue when exposure to UV lamp at 365 nm (Fig. 2c). The UV spectrum of the prepared N-CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. The peak at 205 nm is corresponded to the n-π* transition of the CC or CN bond. The photograph of N-CD dispersion under UV light shows a green color (inset of Fig. 2d).

As shown in Fig. 2e, the fluorescence spectra of K-CDs and N-CDs shift when the excitation wavelength is altered from 240 to 400 nm and a strong fluorescence emission is observed after excitation at 360 nm. The emission spectra are sensitive to the excitation wavelength. A shift in the emission peaks is observed at longer excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2e and f), indicating their excitation-dependent emission behavior, which implies the successful synthesize of CDs [41–43]. The excitation-dependent emission action of the CDs could be on account of the oxidation of the appearance of the CDs [44].

The antioxidant activities of the prepared CDs are shown in Fig. 3(a). The DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging capacities of K-CDs were 62.1 % and 80.0 %, respectively. The DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging capacities of N-CDs were 75.0 % and 88.2 %, respectively. Thus, both types of CDs exhibit excellent antioxidant activities. This is consistent with the results of the study by Feng et al. [45]. The strong antioxidant activity of the synthesized CDs may be related to the electronegativity contrast between the heteroatom and carbon [46].

The agar well diffusion test was conducted to investigated the antimicrobial properties of CDs against S. aureus and E. coli. As evident in Fig. 3(b), the diameters of the inhibition zones of K-CDs were 12.35 and 6.62 mm, respectively. The diameters of the inhibition zones of N-CDs were 8.33 and 6.00 mm, respectively. The most likely antimicrobial mechanism is the generation of ROS such as O2- and OH- ions and the small size and high surface-to-volume ratio of the CDs, which allows them to accumulate on bacterial surfaces and release free radicals. These activities lead to oxidative stress and cause damage to lipids, nucleotides, and proteins, resulting in cell death [11].

As shown in Fig. 3(c), NCM460 cells are co-incubated with various densities of CDs for 24 h, and the survival rate of the NCM460 cells is >80 % even if the concentration of CDs reaches 30 % in NaAlg, indicating the excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity of the prepared CDs. This consequence demonstrates that the prepared CDs can be effectively applied to hydrogel films.

The functional groups and molecular structures of the AC films were studied using FTIR spectroscopy, and the consequences are shown in Fig. 4(A). A peak at 3271 cm−1 is observed in whole films and is corresponded to the hydroxyl stretching in NaAlg, which is incorporated with the NH stretching in the same region [47]. The bands at 2932 and 2930 cm−1 matches to the CH stretching vibrations of the alkane groups of NaAlg [48]. The peaks at 1630 cm−1 is corresponded to the CC stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1026 cm−1 is corresponded to the C–O–C groups which could be attributed to the successful release of Ca2+ by CDs and realisation of ionic crosslinking with NaAlg in the hydrogel films [49]. The location of the OH peak transforms from 3249 cm−1 to 3296 cm−1 after the addition of CDs into the NaAlg hydrogel film, manifesting the generation of hydrogen bonds between the NaAlg and CDs [50,51]. However, a minor increase in peak intensity is observed in the films with 30 % CDs. It can be on account of the high aggregation of CDs appended to the film [52].

The morphology of the fracture surface of the freeze-dried hydrogel films is shown in Fig. 4(B). All hydrogel films exhibited a three-dimensional network structure. The AC-added hydrogel films show fewer pores on the surface as the CDs content increased, suggesting an increase in the compactness of the NaAlg network and the strength of the hydrogel. However, the number of holes on the surface of the two types of hydrogel films increases when the CDs content is >7.5 %, suggesting that adding too many CDs may affect the structure of the hydrogel adversely.

The dispersal of CDs in solid-state thin films is important for expanding and reinforcing their practical application. The release behaviors of the two types of CDs during the immersion of the AC films in 3 % acetic acid and 95 % ethanol solution for 24 h are shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). The amount of CDs released from the films in two different food simulants presents a nonlinear relationship with release time. At the early stage of soaking, the release rate of CDs from the activated NaAlg film into the simulation solution is fast. Subsequently, the release rate slows down and then remains unchanged after a certain release time. It is found that the actual degree of simulated release mainly depends on the compatibility of CDs with NaAlg and the simulation solution. Better compatibility between the functional ingredients and the simulation solution results in higher solubility and greater release of CDs into the simulation solution [53].

The release of N-CDs is significantly higher than that of K-CDs. The release of CDs in the simulation liquids is fitted with the Korsmeyer–Peppas model using Eq. (3) as shown in Table 1 . Furthermore, the diffusion index obtained through Korsmeyer–Peppas model fitting in 95 % ethanol and 3 % acetic acid simulation solution ranges between 0 and 0.5. The diffusion index increases as the concentration of CDs increases gradually. This result implies that the release mechanism of the prepared CDs in the simulation solution conforms to Fick's second law of diffusion. The diffusion coefficient calculated using Eq. (4) is presented in Table 1. The D value of the CDs in 3 % acetic acid is slightly higher than that in the 95 % ethanol simulants. This result suggests that CDs in both 95 % ethanol and 3 % acetic acid food simulants effectively penetrate into the film, leading to swelling of the film and accelerating the rate of release of functional ingredients. Overall, the actual release of CDs in the simulation solution is consistent, and the larger the diffusion coefficient, the shorter the release time required to reach equilibrium [54].

Table 2 presents the mechanical properties of the prepared hydrogel films. The mechanical properties of the ACk films are better than those of the AC-0 films. In addition, ACk-7.5 exhibits the highest TS (56.74 MPa). The same trend is observed for the ACn hydrogel films. The addition of CDs at concentrations above 30 % leads to a prominent reduction in TS (P < 0.05), maybe on account of the congeries of CDs [41,51]. Javanbakht and Namazi covered that the TS of starch films increased when the content of graphene CDs was <17 %, however, it decreased when the CD content was >17 % [55].

Table 2 presents the gel strength and swelling ratio of the NaAlg hydrogel films. The AC-0 film is dissolved in water and unable to form a hydrogel after swelling. However, the NaAlg hydrogel films with CDs still keep the completeness of the gel structure after swelling for 24 h. This is mainly attributed that the Ca2+ in the system is released in the presence of acidic CDs to promote the cross-linking of NaAlg. The gel strength increases and then decreases with the increase in CD content, and the gel strength is the highest when the CDs content is 7.5 %. All hydrogel films exhibit well swelling ratios between 6777 % and 8452 %. It is worth noting that the value is higher than commercial NaAlg hydrogel films (50 % to 1800 %) [56–58]. This result is attributed to the Ca2+ in the NaAlg/CaCO3 system being released in the presence of acidic CDs to promote the cross-linking of NaAlg.

Fig. 6(a) and (b) show the water absorption rate of AC hydrogel films prepared with different concentrations of CDs. The absorption of distilled water by NaAlg hydrogel films is studied. As shown in Fig. 6(a) and (b), the absorption rate of NaAlg hydrogel decreases slightly with the increase in the concentrations of the two types of CDs. The absorption rate reaches its lowest value when the CD content is 30 %. This may be because, with the increase in CD content, the higher crosslinking strength of the NaAlg hydrogel film decreases the number of channels present, leading to a decrease in the water absorption rate. However, the swelling capacity is still good, and the lower water absorption rate of the NaAlg hydrogel film may help retain nutrients and water in the food system. Thus, the excessive absorption of water from the product can be avoided.

From the DSC analysis in Fig. 7(A) and Table 3 , it can be seen that the melting points (T
m ) of the hydrogel film with added CDs are all higher than those of the pure NaAlg film. The T
m of hydrogel films with added K-CDs and N-CDs do not differ much. The T
m of NaAlg hydrogel film increases gradually with the increase in the number of doped CDs, which indicates that the addition of doped CDs improves the thermal property of NaAlg hydrogel film. The increase in T
m values may be due to hydrogen bonding between NaAlg and CDs as well as the high fusibility of the CDs themselves [36]. The increment in the content of acidic CDs dissolves more Ca2+ in the system, and the formation of inter-component polyelectrolyte interactions enhances the intermolecular interactions of the polymer. This hinders the migration of NaAlg chains, which leads to an increase in melting point [59].

Fig. 7(B) demonstrates the variation of TGA curves of the prepared samples. As can be seen from the figure, all treatment groups shows similar thermal degradation behavior. The stage of thermal decomposition is delayed for all composite films compared to AC-0 film and all samples show two stages of weightlessness. The first stage occurs at 25–150 °C (weight loss of 20 %), which is related to the loss of volatile components and water from the film. The second stage is the main weight loss interval, occurring at 155–250 °C, which may be related to carbon chain decomposition of NaAlg, peptide chain breakage and thermal degradation of glycerol. It is shown that the addition of CDs does not change the thermal decomposition behavior of NaAlg in hydrogel films where sodium alginate dominates [60]. The CD-added film degrades more slowly because the CDs adsorbed moisture. The third step of decomposition of the films with added CDs is slightly different due to the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the biopolymer as well as the surface functionalization of the CDs. The reason for this, as reported by Ezati, Roy et al., is that CDs can entrap water molecules [61].

With the increase in the concentration of CDs, the DPPH radical and ABTS radical scavenging capacity of the NaAlg hydrogel films increases gradually. As evident in Fig. 6(e) and (f), the DPPH radical scavenging capacities of ACk-30 and ACn-30 films are approximately 64.8 % and 75.4 %, respectively. Similarly, the ABTS radical scavenging capacities of ACk-30 and ACn-30 films are as high as 56.5 % and 98 %, respectively. It is found that the antioxidant activity of all CD-incorporated hydrogel films is observably greater than that of the CD-free films. This is because CDs can deactivate DPPH radicals by carrying NH2 electrons, hydrogen, and compositions of both [62]. Gao et al. demonstrated that the prepared Scutellaria barbata-CDs exhibited superior scavenging abilities against multiple reactive oxygen and nitrogen species, including -OH, DPPH, and -NO [63].

The colony count method was created to evaluate the antimicrobial activities of AC-0, AC-5, AC-7.5, AC-15, and AC-30 films against E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 8(a) and (b)). The incorporation of CDs in the films significantly increases their antimicrobial activities against the tested bacteria in a concentration-dependent manner. The growth inhibition rate of E. coli and S. aureus incubates with ACk-30 were 95.66 % and 97.33 % after 24 h, respectively. Similarly, the growth inhibition rates of E. coli and S. aureus incubated with ACn-30 were 94 % and 95.67 % after 24 h, respectively. To confirm the antimicrobial activities of the AC films, their antimicrobial activities are evaluated using the bacterial growth curve method against E. coli and S. aureus as indicated in Fig. 8(c–f). The CD-free film does not show any antimicrobial activity against both tested bacteria, while the CD-incorporated hydrogel films exhibits distinctive antimicrobial activity. The number of surviving bacteria decreases with the increase in CD content, which can be attributed to the antimicrobial activity of the CDs. The CDs can also be combined with other antimicrobial substances to further improve the antimicrobial performance of the NaAlg hydrogel film. Different bacteriostatic agents can be added in subsequent studies to investigate the enhancement of the bacteriostatic ability of the hydrogel film by combined bacteriostasis [64–66].

CDs and N-CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted method, with citric acid, K2HPO4, and urea as carbon, potassium, and nitrogen sources, respectively. By adding CDs, the mechanical properties were improved and it gives the NaAlg hydrogel film excellent antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. The DPPH radical scavenging capacities of ACk-30 and ACn-30 films are approximately 64.8 % and 75.4 %, respectively. Similarly, the ABTS radical scavenging capacities of ACk-30 and ACn-30 films are as high as 56.5 % and 98 %, respectively. The NaAlg hydrogel film preparation process that requires no additional acidic substances and is natural and environmentally friendly. And then the CDs in both 95 % ethanol and 3 % acetic acid food simulants effectively penetrate into the film, leading to swelling of the film and accelerating the rate of release of functional ingredients. Due to its good diffusion properties and antimicrobial and antioxidant properties, it is probable to turn out inexpensive and biodegradable films for packaging foods that are vulnerable to bacterial spoilage. CDs were added to NaAlg/CaCO3 films to research their potential as functional fillers in medical, biological engineering, food preservation, and other fields.

Pengyuan Sun: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Experiment. Xin Li: Investigation, Experiment. Ying-ao Zhu: Investigation & Experiment. Meihui Wang: Investigation & Experiment. Baohua Kong: Conceptualization. Hui Wang: Review & Editing, Conceptualization, Validation. Qian Liu: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32302129), the Natural Science Foundation of Heilongjiang Province (LH2021C048), and the Heilongjiang Postdoctoral Financial Assistance (LBH-Z17214).
